10.note: Look up at the sky and count the stars — if indeed you can count them. So shall your offspring be — Moses's declaration that Israel has become as numerous as the stars of heaven is a direct fulfillment report of the Abrahamic promise. The covenant count (stars of heaven) that Abraham received is now realized at the threshold of the land: the forty-year wilderness generation is itself the evidence of divine faithfulness to the Abrahamic oath.

17.note: My brothers and sisters, believers in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ must not show favoritism — James's prohibition of partiality in the Christian assembly echoes the judicial instruction of Deut 1:17: you shall not show partiality in judgment; hear the small and the great alike. Both texts address the same temptation (differential treatment based on status) and ground the prohibition in the same logic: the judgment ultimately belongs to God, who is no respecter of persons.

31.note: In all their distress he too was distressed, and the angel of his presence saved them. In his love and mercy he redeemed them; he lifted them up and carried them all the days of old — the father-son carrying image of Deut 1:31 (as a man carries his son) is the paradigm that Isaiah develops into the theology of YHWH's own suffering-solidarity with Israel in the wilderness. The carrying is not merely parental tenderness but divine identification with the carried one's burden.

It was I who taught Ephraim to walk, taking them by the arms... I led them with cords of kindness, with bands of love, and I was to them like one who eases the yoke on their jaws — Hosea's retrospect on the exodus uses the father-child carrying and leading imagery that Deut 1:31 articulates. Moses's single image of YHWH carrying Israel like a son becomes in Hosea 11 a sustained portrait of parental attachment and tender instruction.

32.note: Do not harden your hearts as you did in the rebellion, during the time of testing in the desert, where your ancestors tested and tried me — the Kadesh-barnea failure narrated in Deut 1:32-46 (yet in spite of all this you did not trust the LORD) is the specific episode Hebrews holds up as the paradigmatic warning for the new covenant community. The failure to enter the land through unbelief becomes the type of failing to enter God's rest.

35.note: So I declared on oath in my anger: They shall never enter my rest — the oath that bars the faithless generation from the land (Deut 1:35: not one of the men of this wicked generation shall see the good land) is the same oath cited in Ps 95:11 and then in Heb 3:11. The Deuteronomic verdict becomes the Psalmic warning and then the Hebrews eschatological application: the rest still remains for those who trust.

7.note: My God will meet all your needs according to the riches of his glory in Christ Jesus — Paul's promise to the Philippians is grounded in the covenant-provision principle Moses articulates in Deut 2:7: these forty years the LORD your God has been with you; you have not lacked anything. The wilderness provision of YHWH for the full duration of the wilderness period is the OT paradigm for the apostolic confidence that the God who provided in the desert continues to provide in mission.

14.note: And with whom was he angry for forty years? Was it not with those who sinned, whose bodies perished in the wilderness? — the thirty-eight year attrition of the faithless generation (Deut 2:14: until the entire generation of warriors had perished from the camp) is cited in Hebrews as the result of God's anger at unbelief. The generation's physical dying-off becomes in Hebrews a type of the spiritual consequence of persistent rebellion against the covenant word.

25.note: Rahab said to the men: I know that the LORD has given you this land and that a great fear of you has fallen on us — the YHWH-induced terror that Deut 2:25 predicts (I will begin to put the dread and fear of you upon all peoples under heaven) is confirmed by Rahab's testimony in Joshua 2: the Canaanites have heard and their hearts melted. The divine promise of anticipatory terror is fulfilled in the Jericho testimony before any Israelite sword is drawn.

30.note: Therefore God has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy, and he hardens whom he wants to harden — Paul's theological statement about divine sovereignty in Rom 9:18 draws on the hardening pattern that Deut 2:30 illustrates: the LORD your God made Sihon's spirit obstinate and his heart hard. The Pharaoh-hardening logic (already seen in Exodus) recurs here with Sihon; Paul generalizes the pattern into the principle of sovereign divine freedom in mercy and hardening.

24.note: Among the gods there is none like you, Lord; no deeds can compare with yours. All the nations you have made will come and worship before you... for you are great and do marvelous deeds; you alone are God — Moses's prayer in Deut 3:24 (what god in heaven or on earth can do such works as yours?) is the progenitor of the comparative-monotheism formula that Psalm 86 develops. Both texts move from the incomparability of YHWH's deeds to the logical conclusion of his sole deity.

28.note: Be strong and courageous, because you will lead these people to inherit the land I swore to their ancestors — the LORD's commissioning of Joshua in Josh 1 is the direct fulfillment of Moses's command in Deut 3:28: commission Joshua, and encourage him and strengthen him, for he shall cross over at the head of this people. The triple be strong-and-courageous of Joshua 1 fulfills the triple mandate Moses received here to commission, encourage, and strengthen his successor.

2.note: I warn everyone who hears the words of the prophecy of this scroll: if anyone adds anything to them, God will add to that person the plagues described in this scroll... if anyone takes words away from this scroll of prophecy, God will take away from that person any share in the tree of life — Revelation's closing warning against addition or subtraction from its words is the canonical application of the Deuteronomic canonical-sealing formula of 4:2 (do not add to the word I command you, nor take from it) to the final book of the NT canon.

12.note: And the Father who sent me has himself testified concerning me. You have never heard his voice or seen his form — Jesus's statement that no one has heard YHWH's voice or seen his form directly invokes the Sinai theophany of Deut 4:12 (you heard the sound of words but saw no form; there was only a voice) as the paradigmatic divine encounter. Jesus implicitly positions himself as the unprecedented visible form of YHWH: in contrast to Sinai, in him the invisible God has become seeable and hearable in a new mode.

24.note: For our God is a consuming fire — Hebrews quotes Deut 4:24 directly in the context of warning the new covenant community not to treat the gospel's grace as license for indifference. The consuming-fire nature of YHWH is not mitigated in the new covenant; rather, the greater privilege of approaching the heavenly Zion (vs. earthly Sinai) increases rather than decreases the gravity of rejecting the speaking God.

29.note: You will seek me and find me when you seek me with all your heart — Jeremiah's letter to the Babylonian exiles quotes Deut 4:29 nearly verbatim (from there you will seek the LORD your God and you will find him, if you search for him with all your heart). The Mosaic promise of post-scattering restoration through wholehearted seeking becomes Jeremiah's direct pastoral promise to the exiles: the worst-case Deuteronomic scenario is not the end of the covenant.

35.note: I am the LORD, and there is no other; apart from me there is no God... so that from the rising of the sun to the place of its setting people may know there is none besides me — Second Isaiah's sustained monotheistic proclamation (there is no other) is the prophetic extension of the Deuteronomic formula first stated in 4:35: the LORD is God; there is no other besides him. The Deuteronomic exclusive monotheism becomes the theological spine of Isaiah 40-55.

We know that there is no God but one — Paul's practical premise for the idol-food discussion in 1 Corinthians 8 reproduces the Deuteronomic monotheism of 4:35. The heis theos (one God) confession Paul invokes is the Greek articulation of what Moses established at Sinai: the LORD alone is God; no other being shares his divine status, so idols receive no genuine honor when Israel sacrifices.

37.note: You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit — Jesus's reminder of divine initiative in election expresses the same theological logic as Deut 4:37: because he loved your fathers and chose their descendants. In both texts, the initiative belongs entirely to YHWH/Christ; the chosen party does not earn or initiate the relationship but finds itself already loved and appointed.

6.note: I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery — the Decalogue's historical prologue frames divine authority in terms of redemptive act, not raw power. Zechariah's Benedictus uses the same framework: God has rescued us from the hand of our enemies, to serve him without fear, as he promised.

7.note: You shall have no other gods before me — Jesus's response to the devil's offer of all the kingdoms of the world ('worship me') draws on both Deuteronomy 6:13 ('fear the LORD your God and serve him only') and implicitly on the first commandment; exclusive worship of God is the covenant foundation Jesus defends in the wilderness.

The angel's rebuke of John when he falls to worship ('Do not do that! Worship God!') enforces the first commandment's exclusivity. Revelation's insistence on God-alone worship throughout reflects the Deuteronomic foundation laid here.

9.note: The visiting of iniquity on subsequent generations is the same formulation as Exodus 20:5 — the Deuteronomic Decalogue reproduces it verbatim; the NT understanding of this principle is nuanced: Ezek 18 insists on individual accountability, while Jesus's death breaks the inherited-consequence pattern through forgiveness.

16.note: Paul explicitly cites 'Honor your father and mother' as 'the first commandment with a promise' (Eph 6:2), quoting the Deuteronomic form 'that it may go well with you and that you may live long in the land.' The Deuteronomic covenant-land promise attached to the fifth commandment becomes in Paul the promise attached to childhood obedience within the new covenant household.

Jesus cites the fifth commandment (honoring parents) against the Pharisees who allow corban-dedication to override the duty to parents — the commandment's binding force on covenant-community relationships is precisely what Jesus defends.

17.note: You shall not murder — Jesus cites this commandment in the Sermon on the Mount ('You have heard it said to those of old...') and radicalizes it to the level of anger and contempt. The Deuteronomic sixth commandment is the launching point for the first antithesis.

Paul summarizes the social commandments of the Decalogue — including 'You shall not murder' — under the principle 'Love your neighbor as yourself.' Deuteronomy's commandments are the specific content that neighbor-love fulfills.

18.note: You shall not commit adultery — Jesus cites this commandment in the second antithesis of the Sermon on the Mount, radicalizing the seventh commandment from the act of adultery to the inner disposition of lustful looking. The Deuteronomic prohibition is the text Jesus interprets and intensifies.

James cites both 'Do not commit adultery' and 'Do not murder' as examples of commandments from 'him who said' — the single divine lawgiver. Breaking one commandment makes one liable under the whole law; the Decalogue is treated as a unified whole.

21.note: Paul identifies the commandment 'You shall not covet' as the specific Decalogue word that revealed sin in him: 'I would not have known what covetousness was if the law had not said you shall not covet.' The Deuteronomic form of the tenth commandment (which specifically adds 'wife,' 'field,' and 'male servant' to the Exodus list) is the form Paul would have encountered in synagogue catechesis.

Paul includes 'You shall not covet' in his summary of Decalogue commandments that are fulfilled in love of neighbor — the covetousness prohibition is the inward commandment that disciplines desire toward others.

22.note: These words the LORD spoke out of the fire, the cloud, and the thick darkness, with a loud voice — Hebrews 12 explicitly contrasts the Sinai theophany (fire, darkness, trumpet blast, voice) with the approach to Mount Zion through Christ. The Deuteronomic description of the Sinai-voice experience is the OT counterpart to the new covenant gathering Hebrews describes.

29.note: If only they had such a heart as this always, to fear me and keep all my commandments — this divine lament expresses exactly what Moses's heart-circumcision passages (Deut 10:16; 30:6) and Jeremiah's new covenant promise address: the inner renewal that makes the law-keeping God longs for possible. The condition God wishes Israel had becomes, in the new covenant, the condition God creates.

4.note: Jesus cites the Shema as the first and greatest commandment — 'Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one' — in response to a scribe's question (Mark 12:29-30). The Shema provides the theological foundation for exclusive love of God; Jesus presents it not as one commandment among many but as the first command from which all others derive.

Paul restructures the Shema Christologically: 'yet for us there is one God the Father... and one Lord Jesus Christ.' He does not cite Deuteronomy 6:4 explicitly but maps the one-God-one-Lord structure of the Shema onto a two-person formula that places Jesus within the divine identity. This is the most profound early Christological use of the Shema.

You believe that God is one; you do well — James appeals to monotheism (the Shema's content) as something even demons affirm, in order to press past bare intellectual assent to the obedient faith that the Shema demands. The Shema's claim is deployed as the baseline from which genuine faith must exceed.

Hear O Israel the LORD our God the LORD is one — Jesus cites the Shema (Deut 6:4-5) as the first and greatest commandment; the Shema frames the entire law in the context of YHWH's singular Lordship over Israel

5.note: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:5 verbatim as the greatest commandment: 'You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.' Matthew's addition of 'mind' (dianoia) to the Markan 'heart, soul, and strength' may reflect the LXX's alternate rendering. Jesus presents the Shema's love-command as the summary of the whole law.

The lawyer who correctly summarizes the law quotes Deuteronomy 6:5 together with Leviticus 19:18 — the same pairing Jesus makes in Matthew 22:37-39. Jesus affirms the summary and connects it to the parable of the Good Samaritan, applying Deuteronomy's love-of-God to the question of who qualifies as neighbor.

13.note: You shall fear the LORD your God. You shall serve him — Jesus quotes the second half of Deut 6:13 in response to the devil's temptation to worship him: 'It is written: worship the Lord your God and serve him only.' Where Israel in the wilderness failed the test of exclusive loyalty to YHWH (golden calf, Baalism), Jesus as the true Israel restores covenant faithfulness by refusing false worship.
Worship the Lord your God only — Jesus quotes in temptation

16.note: You shall not put the LORD your God to the test, as you tested him at Massah — Jesus quotes Deut 6:16 in response to the devil's temptation to jump from the Temple pinnacle and force divine rescue: 'It is also written: do not put the Lord your God to the test.' Israel demanded miraculous proof at Massah (Exod 17:7); Jesus refuses to demand proof from God, trusting the Father without coercion.
Do not put the Lord to test — Jesus quotes in temptation

20.note: When your son asks what is the meaning of the testimonies and statutes — the catechetical pattern of parental explanation of God's saving acts anticipates the NT call to give a defense (apologia) to everyone who asks about the hope within. Both texts envision a questioning outsider and a community prepared to explain the meaning of its identity and practices.

6.note: You are a people holy to the LORD your God; the LORD has chosen you to be his treasured possession (segullah) — Peter applies all three designations directly to the church: 'you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession' (combining Exod 19:5-6 with Deut 7:6). Israel's election-vocabulary is transferred to the new covenant community without remainder; the church is the people of God whose identity is defined by the Deuteronomic election.

Christ gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness and to purify for himself a people who are his own possession (periousion) — Paul's word periousion is the LXX translation of segullah (treasured possession) in Deut 7:6. The Deuteronomic election of Israel as God's special possession is reapplied to the church purchased by Christ's blood.

7.note: The LORD did not set his love on you because you were more numerous — you were the smallest of all peoples — the paradox of God choosing the numerically insignificant is the OT ground for Paul's theology of divine election by grace: God chose the foolish, weak, and lowly things of the world to shame the strong, so that no one may boast before him. The Deuteronomic logic of election-against-greatness is generalized by Paul to the cross-shaped community.

8.note: Because the LORD loved you and was keeping the oath he swore to your fathers — Ephesians 1:4-5 grounds election in identical terms: chosen in love before the foundation of the world, predestined according to the pleasure of his will. The Deuteronomic grounding of Israel's election in divine love and prior oath (not human merit) is the OT pattern that Paul's theology of grace-election continues and universalizes.

The principle 'God's purpose in election might stand, not based on works but on him who calls' — Paul's discussion of the divine election of Jacob over Esau applies the same Deuteronomic logic: God chose the lesser before any works were done, in order to demonstrate that election is of grace, not human advantage.

9.note: Know that the LORD your God is God — the faithful God who keeps his covenant and steadfast love — Paul's foundational assertion 'God is faithful' (1 Cor 1:9; 10:13; 2 Cor 1:18) draws on this Deuteronomic characterization of God as the covenant-keeping faithful one. Divine faithfulness is not a NT innovation but the Deuteronomic core of Israel's covenant theology.

If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive — the divine faithfulness that grounds the promise of forgiveness in 1 John is the same quality that Deuteronomy 7:9 names as definitional for YHWH: he is the faithful God who keeps his covenant. The character that Deuteronomy declares is the basis on which NT forgiveness-promises are grounded.

25.note: Do not covet the silver or gold on the carved images — nothing abominable or detestable (toevah) was to enter Israel's covenant space; Revelation 21:27 declares that nothing abominable (bdelykton — the LXX translation of toevah) will enter the new Jerusalem. Deuteronomy's covenant-purity requirement becomes the criterion for the eschatological city.

3.note: Jesus answered, It is written: Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God — Jesus quotes Deut 8:3 verbatim in response to the first wilderness temptation. The manna-testing episode (YHWH humbled Israel by letting them hunger, then fed them to teach dependence on the divine word) becomes the script Jesus enacts perfectly: where Israel failed the bread test, Jesus trusts YHWH's provision and word.

I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry — the manna theology of Deut 8:3 (the bread of the divine word) reaches its Christological climax in John 6. Jesus is not merely the teacher of the divine word but is himself the living bread that came down from heaven, surpassing and fulfilling the manna that Moses mediated.
Man shall not live by bread alone — Jesus quotes in temptation

5.note: Endure hardship as discipline; God is treating you as his children. For what children are not disciplined by their father? — Hebrews develops the father-son discipline principle that Deuteronomy 8:5 establishes as the interpretive framework for covenant suffering. Moses's explanation that YHWH's testing-in-the-wilderness is paternal discipline (as a man disciplines his son) becomes the NT rationale for the suffering of the people of God: it is not punishment but formation.

17.note: What do you have that you did not receive? And if you did receive it, why do you boast as though you did not? — Paul's anti-boasting challenge in 1 Corinthians 4 is the NT application of the Deuteronomic warning: beware of saying in your heart, My power and the strength of my hands produced this wealth for me. Both texts diagnose the same spiritual error: attributing to the creature what belongs to the Creator.

4.note: For by grace you have been saved, through faith — and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God — not by works, so that no one can boast — Paul's foundational statement of salvation by grace and not works is the NT theological articulation of the principle Moses establishes in Deut 9:4-6: do not say in your heart that my righteousness brought me into the land. The inheritance is given by grace (confirmed covenant) not earned by merit; the NT applies the same principle to the new covenant.

9.note: After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him — Jesus's forty-day fast in the wilderness recapitulates the Mosaic forty-day fast in Deut 9:9 (I stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights, eating no bread and drinking no water). As Moses received the Torah-tablets after his fast, Jesus demonstrates his covenant faithfulness under testing. Where Moses's intercession followed Israel's failure, Jesus succeeds where Israel failed.

26.note: Did God reject his people? By no means! I am an Israelite myself, a descendant of Abraham, from the tribe of Benjamin. God did not reject his people, whom he foreknew — Paul's rhetorical denial of Israel's permanent rejection draws on the same logic Moses uses in Deut 9:26-29: O Lord GOD, do not destroy your people and your inheritance. Moses appeals to the Abrahamic covenant and YHWH's reputation; Paul appeals to divine foreknowledge and election. Both resist the conclusion that YHWH has abandoned Israel.

12.note: He has shown you, O mortal, what is good. And what does the LORD require of you? To act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with your God — Micah's prophetic summary of the covenant requirement echoes the rhetorical structure of Deut 10:12 (what does the LORD your God ask of you except to fear him, walk in his ways, love him, serve him with all your heart and soul). Both texts answer the question what does YHWH really want? by cutting through ritual specifics to describe a whole-life covenant orientation.

16.note: Circumcise yourselves to the LORD, circumcise your hearts and do not let your hearts become uncircumcised — Jeremiah's call for circumcision of the heart is the direct prophetic extension of Moses's command in Deut 10:16. What Deuteronomy commands as a spiritual-ethical imperative (circumcise your hearts and stop being stubborn), Jeremiah reissues as an eschatological urgency before judgment; the same command runs through the prophets until it becomes a divine promise (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:26).

Circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code — Paul's theological conclusion on circumcision in Romans 2 is the NT fulfillment of the Deuteronomic command of Deut 10:16. What Moses commanded as a human imperative and Jeremiah announced as an eschatological requirement, Paul declares as accomplished by the Spirit: the circumcised heart that Deuteronomy demanded is given by God in the new covenant.

17.note: They will wage war against the Lamb, but the Lamb will triumph over them because he is Lord of lords and King of kings — the divine title Lord of lords that Deuteronomy 10:17 applies to YHWH (the LORD your God is God of gods and Lord of lords) is transferred in Revelation to Jesus the Lamb. The exclusive sovereignty that belongs to YHWH in Deut 10:17 is the same sovereignty Christ exercises in the eschatological battle; the Lamb is identified with the LORD by sharing his unique title.

18.note: Religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress — James's definition of pure religion directly applies the Deuteronomic ethic of Deut 10:18 (YHWH upholds justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the foreigner). The social priorities that flow from YHWH's own character (he defends the fatherless, widow, and stranger) become the defining marks of authentic covenant community for James.

15.note: A prophet like me will the LORD your God raise up for you — Peter cites Deut 18:15 as fulfilled in Jesus; the eschatological prophet-like-Moses was the figure Israel expected, and Peter declares Jesus to be that prophet

God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers — Stephen's speech identifies the prophet-like-Moses promise as the Christological center of Moses's ministry; Israel's rejection of Moses typifies their rejection of Jesus
The Prophet who is to come into the world
Every matter established by two or three witnesses

23.note: Cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree — Paul cites Deut 21:23 as fulfilled in the crucifixion: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree; the cross is the site of curse-absorption
Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree

1.note: Give a certificate of divorce -- Jesus tightens the standard

4.note: Do not muzzle an ox while it treads out grain
Do not muzzle an ox -- the worker deserves his wages

26.note: Cursed is everyone who does not continue in the law

12.note: Do not say in your heart who will go up to heaven — Paul adapts Deut 30:12-14 Christologically: the word that is near you, in your heart and mouth, is the word of faith we proclaim; what Deuteronomy said of the Torah-command is now said of Christ and his gospel
The word is near you — righteousness by faith

6.note: Never will I leave you; never will I forsake you

21.note: I will make you jealous of those who are not a nation — Paul cites the Song of Moses (Deut 32:21) as the OT basis for the Gentile mission provoking Israel to jealousy; the unexpected reversal of Gentile blessing is Moses's own warning
I will make you envious by a nation not a nation

35.note: It is mine to avenge; I will repay

43.note: Rejoice O Gentiles with his people — Paul cites Deut 32:43 LXX as one of four OT texts (Rom 15:9-12) proving that Gentile inclusion in the worship of God was always the divine plan from Moses through the Psalms and Isaiah
Rejoice, you Gentiles, with his people
